meta.source_paper: academic cross-chain deanonymization research (e.g., arxiv 2208.02370), bridge protocol analysis

risk_domain.name: Cross-Chain Identity Linkage via Bridge Usage
risk_domain.description: Detects privacy-breaking patterns in L1-to-L2 bridge usage that allow an observer to link addresses across chains, defeating the isolation that separate chains should provide

capabilities: full on-chain observation across L1 and multiple L2s
timing analysis on bridge deposits and withdrawals
amount correlation across chains with fee awareness
address clustering on each chain independently
limitations: no access to sequencer mempool (if private)

H1_same_address_bridge.name: Same address bridge
H1_same_address_bridge.description: User bridges assets using the same address on both L1 and L2, creating a trivial deterministic link. Most canonical bridges default to sending to the same address, making this the most common and most damaging linkage pattern.

signals.name: address_reuse
signals.description: Bridge deposit on L1 and withdrawal on L2 use the identical address (canonical bridge default behavior)

signals.name: ens_cross_chain
signals.description: L1 and L2 addresses both resolve to the same ENS name or are in the same ENS ownership graph

signals.name: create2_deterministic
signals.description: Smart contract wallet deployed via CREATE2 at the same address on both chains, linking via deterministic deployment

detection.threshold: address_reuse is an immediate and complete linkage -- no mitigation possible after the fact

recommendations.description: Generate a fresh address on the destination L2 and specify it as the bridge recipient instead of using the default same-address behavior
Use a liquidity-network bridge (Hop, Across) that routes through liquidity pools rather than direct deposit-withdrawal pairs
Bridge to an intermediate address, wait, then transfer to the intended L2 address via multiple hops

H1_same_address_bridge.fundamental_limitation: If the user has already bridged with the same address, the linkage is permanent and on-chain. No retroactive fix exists -- the only mitigation is abandoning both addresses and starting fresh.

benchmark_scenario.setup: Dataset of N bridge transactions from canonical bridges (Arbitrum, Optimism, zkSync) with known same-address and different-address patterns
benchmark_scenario.metric: 100% detection rate for same-address bridges, false positive rate for CREATE2 matches
benchmark_scenario.baseline: majority of canonical bridge users use same address on both chains

H2_amount_correlation.name: Amount correlation
H2_amount_correlation.description: A bridge deposit on L1 matches a withdrawal on L2 by exact amount (minus predictable fees) within a narrow time window. Even with different addresses, unique or precise amounts create a strong statistical link.

signals.name: exact_amount_match
signals.description: L2 withdrawal amount equals L1 deposit minus the bridge fee, within a time window matching the bridge finality period

signals.name: rare_amount
signals.description: The bridged amount has fewer than 3 matches in the bridge deposit pool within 24 hours

signals.name: precise_amount
signals.description: Amount has more than 4 significant decimal places (not a round number), making it nearly unique

detection.threshold: exact_amount_match AND rare_amount triggers WARNING; all three signals together trigger BLOCK

recommendations.description: Split a single bridge transfer into multiple round-number transfers across different time windows
Round bridge amount to a common denomination (0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 10.0 ETH) to increase the anonymity set
Use liquidity-network bridges where output amounts are determined by pool state, not input amounts

H2_amount_correlation.fundamental_limitation: Amount correlation weakens as bridge volume increases (larger anonymity set), but for low-volume bridges or large amounts, even round numbers may be unique in a given time window. The adversary's ability depends on bridge traffic patterns.

benchmark_scenario.setup: Historical bridge deposit/withdrawal pairs from Arbitrum and Optimism canonical bridges over 30 days. Adversary attempts amount+timing matching.
benchmark_scenario.metric: fraction of bridge transfers where adversary correctly links L1 deposit to L2 withdrawal using amount and timing alone
benchmark_scenario.baseline: unique amounts are near-certain matches; round amounts depend on bridge volume

H3_bridge_sequence_fingerprint.name: Bridge sequence fingerprint
H3_bridge_sequence_fingerprint.description: A user who bridges to the same set of L2s in a recognizable order or pattern creates a behavioral fingerprint. If an address bridges to Arbitrum, then Optimism, then zkSync in a consistent sequence, this pattern is highly identifying even if different addresses are used on each chain.

signals.name: chain_sequence_match
signals.description: L1 address bridges to the same ordered set of L2 chains as another known address cluster

signals.name: timing_cadence
signals.description: Bridge events follow a periodic cadence (e.g., weekly, monthly) that matches a known user's pattern

signals.name: multi_chain_presence
signals.description: Same address or cluster appears on 3+ L2s within a short time window, suggesting systematic bridging

detection.threshold: chain_sequence_match AND timing_cadence triggers WARNING; add multi_chain_presence for higher confidence

recommendations.description: Vary the order and timing of bridges to different L2s to break recognizable patterns
Add random delays (hours to days) between bridges to different chains to prevent cadence fingerprinting
Use different bridge protocols for different L2s to fragment the observable pattern

H3_bridge_sequence_fingerprint.fundamental_limitation: Behavioral fingerprinting is inherently probabilistic and depends on the user population. With few multi-chain users, any multi-chain activity is identifying regardless of countermeasures. As L2 adoption grows, the anonymity set improves naturally.

benchmark_scenario.setup: Simulated population of N users with varying multi-chain bridge patterns. Adversary observes all L1 bridge events and L2 activity.
benchmark_scenario.metric: adversary accuracy in linking L1 identities to L2 activity sets using bridge sequence alone
benchmark_scenario.baseline: depends on population size and chain diversity -- small populations are highly vulnerable

H4_gas_funding_post_bridge.name: Gas funding post-bridge
H4_gas_funding_post_bridge.description: After bridging to an L2, the user's new L2 address receives its first gas funding from bridged ETH traceable to a known L1 address. This creates a direct funding chain that links the L2 identity to the L1 identity even if different addresses were used for the bridge itself.

signals.name: direct_funding_chain
signals.description: L2 address's first ETH transfer comes from an address that received bridge withdrawals from a known L1 address

signals.name: single_hop_funding
signals.description: L2 address funded through exactly one intermediary that itself was funded by a bridge withdrawal

signals.name: funding_timing
signals.description: L2 address funded within minutes of a bridge withdrawal completing, suggesting automated pipeline

detection.threshold: direct_funding_chain triggers BLOCK; single_hop_funding AND funding_timing triggers WARNING

recommendations.description: Fund gas on L2 via a faucet, paymaster, or gasless transaction mechanism to avoid direct funding from bridged assets
Fund the L2 address from an independent source (CEX withdrawal, separate bridge session with delay) before using bridged assets
Wait until the L2 address has received funds from multiple independent sources before transacting, muddying the funding chain

H4_gas_funding_post_bridge.fundamental_limitation: On L2s with low activity, any funding event is observable and the graph is sparse enough to trace even through multiple hops. The gas funding problem is fundamentally the same as for stealth addresses -- someone has to pay for gas, and that payment is visible.

benchmark_scenario.setup: N L2 addresses with varying funding patterns: direct from bridge, via intermediary, from CEX, from faucet
benchmark_scenario.metric: fraction of L2 addresses linkable to an L1 identity via funding chain analysis
benchmark_scenario.baseline: most L2 users fund gas directly from bridged ETH

H5_nft_token_bridge_linkage.name: NFT/token bridge linkage
H5_nft_token_bridge_linkage.description: Bridging unique tokens (NFTs, tokens with non-fungible amounts, rare ERC-20s with very few holders) creates a deterministic link because the token itself is an identifier. If only one Bored Ape #1234 exists across all chains, bridging it links the source and destination addresses with certainty.

signals.name: nft_bridge
signals.description: An ERC-721 or ERC-1155 token with a unique ID was bridged, creating a deterministic cross-chain link

signals.name: rare_token_bridge
signals.description: An ERC-20 token with fewer than 100 holders was bridged, making the transfer highly identifying

signals.name: unique_amount_token
signals.description: A token amount so specific (e.g., 1,337.42069 USDC) that it effectively acts as an identifier across chains

detection.threshold: nft_bridge triggers immediate BLOCK (deterministic link); rare_token_bridge triggers WARNING

recommendations.description: Do not bridge NFTs directly if cross-chain privacy is needed. Sell on L1 and rebuy on L2 through a marketplace.
recommendations.user_cost: high (marketplace fees, price risk, not the same token)

recommendations.description: Only bridge high-volume fungible tokens (ETH, USDC, DAI) in round amounts to maximize anonymity set
Before bridging, check the token's holder count and recent bridge volume to assess linkability risk

H5_nft_token_bridge_linkage.fundamental_limitation: Unique tokens are inherently non-private to move across chains. No amount of operational security can hide the fact that a specific NFT moved from address A on L1 to address B on L2. The only mitigation is not bridging unique assets.

benchmark_scenario.setup: Bridge event dataset including NFT bridges, rare token bridges, and high-volume fungible token bridges
benchmark_scenario.metric: 100% detection rate for NFT bridges; precision on rare token classification
benchmark_scenario.baseline: any NFT bridge is a certain link; rare tokens depend on holder count threshold

bridge_monitor.description: Monitor bridge contract events across L1 and supported L2s to track deposit/withdrawal pairs, token types, and volumes

address_separator.description: Generate fresh addresses on destination chains and maintain a private mapping to avoid on-chain address reuse

amount_splitter.description: Split a bridge transfer into multiple round-number amounts across different time windows to maximize anonymity set

combined_benchmark.description: End-to-end benchmark measuring cross-chain linkability with all heuristics applied

methodology.step_1: Collect bridge transaction dataset across Arbitrum, Optimism, zkSync canonical bridges (N >= 10000 pairs)
methodology.step_2: Label ground truth linkages using known same-address bridges as positive examples
methodology.step_3: Run all 5 heuristics against each bridge event
methodology.step_5: Apply profile recommendations to a subset and re-measure
methodology.step_6: Compare: linkability rate before vs after countermeasures

combined_benchmark.target: reduce cross-chain linkability from estimated 80%+ to <15% with all recommendations applied

adversary_levels.name: basic
adversary_levels.description: Address reuse + unique token linkage only

templates.risk_assessment_block: BLOCKED: Bridge transaction creates {heuristic_name} linkage with {confidence}% confidence. {signal_description}. This bridge would link your L1 and L2 identities.
templates.summary: Analyzed {n_bridges} bridge transactions. {n_linkable} create cross-chain linkages ({pct_linkable}%). Top risks: {top_risks}.
templates.summary_clean: All {n_bridges} bridge transactions pass privacy checks. No cross-chain linkage detected.
templates.skill_suggestion: To mitigate {heuristic_name}, I can {skill_action}. This reduces linkability by ~{effectiveness_pct}%. {user_cost_note}
